question:
Let $(x_1,\,x_2,\,\ldots\,x_n)$ be a point chosen at random from the
$n$-dimensional region defined by
$0<x_1<x_2<\cdots < x_n<1.$  Let $f$ be a continuous function on
$[0,1]$ with $f(1)=0$.
Set $x_0=0$ and $x_{n+1}=1$. Show that the expected value of the
Riemann sum
\[
\sum_{i=0}^n (x_{i+1}-x_i) f(x_{i+1})
\]
is $\int_0^1 f(t)P(t)\, dt$, where $P$ is a polynomial of degree $n$,
independent of $f$, with $0\le P(t)\le 1$ for $0\le t \le 1$.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
solution:
Solution 1. We may drop the \( i=n \) term in the Riemann sum, since \( f\left(x_{n+1}\right)= \) \( f(1)=0 \). The volume of the region \( 0<x_{1}<\cdots<x_{n}<1 \) in \( \mathbb{R}^{n} \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{x_{n}} \cdots \int_{0}^{x_{2}} d x_{1} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{n}=1 / n!
\]

Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is \( E=\left(\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} M_{i}\right) /(1 / n!) \), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
M_{i} & =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{x_{n}} \cdots \int_{0}^{x_{2}}\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i}\right) f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{1} \cdots d x_{n} \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{x_{n}} \cdots \int_{0}^{x_{i+1}}\left(x_{i+1}-x_{i}\right) \frac{x_{i}^{i-1}}{(i-1)!} f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{i} d x_{i+1} \cdots d x_{n} \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{x_{n}} \cdots \int_{0}^{x_{i+2}}\left(x_{i+1} \frac{x_{i+1}^{i}}{i!}-\frac{x_{i+1}^{i+1}}{(i+1) \cdot(i-1)!}\right) f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{i+1} \cdots d x_{n} \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{x_{n}} \cdots \int_{0}^{x_{i+2}} \frac{x_{i+1}^{i+1}}{(i+1)!} f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{i+1} \cdots d x_{n} \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{x_{i+1}}^{1} \int_{x_{i+1}}^{x_{n}} \int_{x_{i+1}}^{x_{i+3}} \frac{x_{i+1}^{i+1}}{(i+1)!} f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{i+2} \cdots d x_{n} d x_{i+1} \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(1-x_{i+1}\right)^{n-(i+1)}}{(n-(i+1))!} \frac{x_{i+1}^{i+1}}{(i+1)!} f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{i+1} \\
& =\frac{1}{n!} \int_{0}^{1}\binom{n}{i+1}\left(1-x_{i+1}\right)^{n-(i+1)} x_{i+1}^{i+1} f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{i+1} .
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
E & =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\binom{n}{i+1}\left(1-x_{i+1}\right)^{n-(i+1)} x_{i+1}^{i+1}\right) f\left(x_{i+1}\right) d x_{i+1} \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{j=1}^{n}\binom{n}{j}(1-t)^{n-j} t^{j}\right) f(t) d t \quad\left(\text { we set } j=i+1 \text { and } t=x_{i+1}\right) \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(1-(1-t)^{n}\right) f(t) d t
\end{aligned}
\]
since the sum is the binomial expansion of \( ((1-t)+t)^{n} \) except that the \( j=0 \) term, which is \( (1-t)^{n} \), is missing. Hence \( E=\int_{0}^{1} f(t) P(t) d t \) where \( P(t)=1-(1-t)^{n} \). Clearly \( 0 \leq P(t) \leq 1 \) for \( 0 \leq t \leq 1 \).

Solution 2. Fix \( n \geq 0 \) and let \( \left(y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right) \) be chosen uniformly from \( [0,1]^{n} \). Fix \( t \in[0,1] \), and let \( E_{n}(t) \) denote the expected value of
\[
R_{n}=t-\max \left(\{0\} \cup\left(\left\{y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right\} \cap[0, t]\right)\right) .
\]

Let \( \left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right) \) be the permutation of \( \left(y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}\right) \) such that \( x_{1} \leq \cdots \leq x_{n} \). The distribution of \( \left(x_{1}, \ldots, x_{n}\right) \) equals the distribution in the problem. Define
\[
S=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}\left(y_{i+1}-z_{i+1}\right) f\left(y_{i+1}\right)
\]
where \( z_{i+1} \) is the largest \( y_{j} \) less than \( y_{i+1} \), or 0 if no such \( y_{j} \) exists. The terms in \( S \) are a permutation of those in the original Riemann sum (ignoring the final term in the Riemann sum, which is zero), so the expected values of the Riemann sum and of \( S \) are equal. Each term in \( S \) has expected value \( \int_{0}^{1} E_{n-1}(t) f(t) d t \), since the expected value of \( y_{i+1}-z_{i+1} \) conditioned on the event \( y_{i+1}=t \) for some \( t \in[0,1] \) is the definition of \( E_{n-1}(t) \) using \( \left(y_{j}\right)_{j \neq i+1} \in[0,1]^{n-1} \). Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is \( n \int_{0}^{1} E_{n-1}(t) f(t) d t \).

It remains to determine \( E_{n}(t) \) for \( n \geq 0 \). Let \( y_{n+1} \) be chosen uniformly in \( [0,1] \), independently of the \( y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n} \) in the definition of \( E_{n}(t) \). Then \( E_{n}(t) \) equals the probability that \( y_{n+1} \) is in \( [0, t] \) and is closer to \( t \) than any other \( y_{j} \), since this probability conditioned on a choice of \( y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n} \) equals \( R_{n} \). On the other hand, this probability equals \( \left(1-(1-t)^{n+1}\right) /(n+1) \), since the probability that at least one of \( y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n+1} \) lies in \( [0, t] \) equals \( 1-(1-t)^{n+1} \), and conditioned on this, the probability that the \( y_{j} \) in \( [0, t] \) closest to \( t \) is \( y_{n+1} \) is \( 1 /(n+1) \), since all possible indices for this closest \( y_{j} \) are equally likely. Thus \( E_{n}(t)=\left(1-(1-t)^{n+1}\right) /(n+1) \), and we may take \( P(t)=n E_{n-1}(t)=1-(1-t)^{n} \).

Literature note. For more on Riemann sums, see [Spv, Ch. 13, Appendix 1].

descriptive_long.question:
Let $(firstpoint,\,secondpoint,\,\ldots\,nthpoint)$ be a point chosen at random from the
$dimcount$-dimensional region defined by
$0<firstpoint<secondpoint<\cdots < nthpoint<1.$  Let $contifunc$ be a continuous function on
$[0,1]$ with $contifunc(1)=0$.
Set $initialpoint=0$ and $finalpoint=1$. Show that the expected value of the
Riemann sum
\[
\sum_{i=0}^{dimcount} (nextpoint-indexpoint)\,contifunc(nextpoint)
\]
is $\int_0^1 contifunc(timevalue)\,polyfunc(timevalue)\, d timevalue$, where $polyfunc$ is a polynomial of degree $dimcount$,
independent of $contifunc$, with $0\le polyfunc(timevalue)\le 1$ for $0\le timevalue \le 1$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution 1. We may drop the \( i=dimcount \) term in the Riemann sum, since \( contifunc\left(finalpoint\right)=contifunc(1)=0 \). The volume of the region \( 0<firstpoint<\cdots<nthpoint<1 \) in \( \mathbb{R}^{dimcount} \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{nthpoint} \cdots \int_{0}^{secondpoint}\, d firstpoint\, d secondpoint \cdots d nthpoint = 1/ dimcount!.
\]
Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is
\[
expectval = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{dimcount-1} midterm}{1/ dimcount!},
\]
where
\[
\begin{aligned}
midterm &= \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{nthpoint} \cdots \int_{0}^{secondpoint}\bigl(nextpoint-indexpoint\bigr)\,contifunc\bigl(nextpoint\bigr)\, d firstpoint \cdots d nthpoint\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{nthpoint} \cdots \int_{0}^{nextpoint}\bigl(nextpoint-indexpoint\bigr)\frac{indexpoint^{i-1}}{(i-1)!}\,contifunc(nextpoint)\, d indexpoint\, d nextpoint \cdots d nthpoint\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{nthpoint} \cdots \int_{0}^{secondnextpoint}\Bigl(nextpoint\frac{nextpoint^{i}}{i!}-\frac{nextpoint^{i+1}}{(i+1)\,(i-1)!}\Bigr)\,contifunc(nextpoint)\, d nextpoint \cdots d nthpoint\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{nthpoint} \cdots \int_{0}^{secondnextpoint} \frac{nextpoint^{i+1}}{(i+1)!}\,contifunc(nextpoint)\, d nextpoint \cdots d nthpoint\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \int_{nextpoint}^{1} \int_{nextpoint}^{nthpoint} \int_{nextpoint}^{thirdnextpoint} \frac{nextpoint^{i+1}}{(i+1)!}\,contifunc(nextpoint)\, d secondnextpoint \cdots d nthpoint\, d nextpoint\\
&= \int_{0}^{1} \frac{(1-nextpoint)^{dimcount-(i+1)}}{(dimcount-(i+1))!}\,\frac{nextpoint^{i+1}}{(i+1)!}\,contifunc(nextpoint)\, d nextpoint\\
&= \frac1{dimcount!}\int_{0}^{1}\binom{dimcount}{i+1}(1-nextpoint)^{dimcount-(i+1)} nextpoint^{i+1}\,contifunc(nextpoint)\, d nextpoint.
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
expectval &= \int_{0}^{1}\Bigl(\sum_{i=0}^{dimcount-1}\binom{dimcount}{i+1}(1-nextpoint)^{dimcount-(i+1)} nextpoint^{i+1}\Bigr)\,contifunc(nextpoint)\, d nextpoint\\
&= \int_{0}^{1}\Bigl(\sum_{altindex=1}^{dimcount}\binom{dimcount}{altindex}(1-timevalue)^{dimcount-altindex} timevalue^{altindex}\Bigr)\,contifunc(timevalue)\, d timevalue\\
&= \int_{0}^{1}\bigl(1-(1-timevalue)^{dimcount}\bigr)\,contifunc(timevalue)\, d timevalue,
\end{aligned}
\]
since the sum is the binomial expansion of \(((1-timevalue)+timevalue)^{dimcount}\) with the \(altindex=0\) term \((1-timevalue)^{dimcount}\) omitted. Thus we have
\[
expectval = \int_{0}^{1} contifunc(timevalue)\,\polyfunc(timevalue)\, d timevalue,\qquad \text{where } \polyfunc(timevalue)=1-(1-timevalue)^{dimcount}.
\]
Clearly $0\le \polyfunc(timevalue)\le 1$ for $0\le timevalue\le1$.

Solution 2. Fix $dimcount\ge0$ and let $(ysampleone,\ldots,ysamplen)$ be chosen uniformly from $[0,1]^{dimcount}$.  Fix $timevalue\in[0,1]$, and let $expectn(timevalue)$ denote the expected value of
\[
riemannrand = timevalue-\max\bigl(\{0\}\cup\bigl(\{ysampleone,\ldots,ysamplen\}\cap[0,timevalue]\bigr)\bigr).
\]
Let $(firstpoint,\ldots,nthpoint)$ be the permutation of $(ysampleone,\ldots,ysamplen)$ with $firstpoint\le\cdots\le nthpoint$.  This has the same distribution as in the problem.  Define
\[
sumall = \sum_{i=0}^{dimcount-1}(ysamplenext-zprev)\,contifunc(ysamplenext),
\]
where $zprev$ is the largest $y_{j}$ less than $ysamplenext$, or $0$ if none exists.  The terms in $\sumall$ form a permutation of those in the original Riemann sum (the last term is zero), so the expected values of the two sums coincide.  Each term in $\sumall$ has expected value $\int_{0}^{1}expectprev(timevalue)\,contifunc(timevalue)\,d timevalue$, because, conditioning on $ysamplenext=timevalue$, the expectation of $ysamplenext-zprev$ is exactly $expectprev(timevalue)$ with the other $dimcount-1$ sample points.  Consequently the expected value of the Riemann sum is
\[
 dimcount\int_{0}^{1}expectprev(timevalue)\,contifunc(timevalue)\,d timevalue.
\]
It remains to compute $expectn(timevalue)$ for $dimcount\ge0$.  Let $ysampleextra$ be chosen uniformly from $[0,1]$, independently of the previous points.  Then $expectn(timevalue)$ equals the probability that $ysampleextra\in[0,timevalue]$ and is closer to $timevalue$ than any other $y_{j}$.  This probability is
\[
\frac{1-(1-timevalue)^{dimcount+1}}{dimcount+1},
\]
for the numerator is the probability that at least one of the $dimcount+1$ points lies in $[0,timevalue]$, and, conditional on this, each of the $dimcount+1$ indices is equally likely to be the closest.  Hence
\[
expectn(timevalue)=\frac{1-(1-timevalue)^{dimcount+1}}{dimcount+1},\qquad \text{so } \polyfunc(timevalue)=dimcount\,expectprev(timevalue)=1-(1-timevalue)^{dimcount}.
\]
Literature note.  For more on Riemann sums, see [Spv, Ch.�A0 13, Appendix 1].

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $(butterfly,\,watermelon,\,\ldots\,orangutang)$ be a point chosen at random from the
$pinecone$-dimensional region defined by
$0<butterfly<watermelon<\cdots < orangutang<1.$  Let $riverbank$ be a continuous function on
$[0,1]$ with $riverbank(1)=0$.
Set $lighthouse=0$ and $storyboard=1$. Show that the expected value of the
Riemann sum
\[
\sum_{compassrose=0}^{pinecone} (toothpick-chandelier) riverbank(toothpick)
\]
is $\int_0^1 riverbank(peppermint)blacksmith(peppermint)\, dpeppermint$, where $blacksmith$ is a polynomial of degree $pinecone$,
independent of $riverbank$, with $0\le blacksmith(peppermint)\le 1$ for $0\le peppermint \le 1$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution 1. We may drop the \( compassrose=pinecone \) term in the Riemann sum, since \( riverbank\left(storyboard\right)= \) \( riverbank(1)=0 \). The volume of the region \( 0<butterfly<\cdots<orangutang<1 \) in \( \mathbb{R}^{pinecone} \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{orangutang} \cdots \int_{0}^{watermelon} d butterfly d watermelon \cdots d orangutang=1 / pinecone!
\]

Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is \( whirlwind=\left(\sum_{compassrose=0}^{pinecone-1} playhouse\right) /(1 / pinecone!) \), where
\[
\begin{aligned}
playhouse & =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{orangutang} \cdots \int_{0}^{watermelon}\left(toothpick-chandelier\right) riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d butterfly \cdots d orangutang \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{orangutang} \cdots \int_{0}^{toothpick}\left(toothpick-chandelier\right) \frac{chandelier^{compassrose-1}}{(compassrose-1)!} riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d chandelier d toothpick \cdots d orangutang \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{orangutang} \cdots \int_{0}^{campfire}\left(toothpick \frac{toothpick^{compassrose}}{compassrose!}-\frac{toothpick^{compassrose+1}}{(compassrose+1) \cdot(compassrose-1)!}\right) riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d toothpick \cdots d orangutang \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{orangutang} \cdots \int_{0}^{campfire} \frac{toothpick^{compassrose+1}}{(compassrose+1)!} riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d toothpick \cdots d orangutang \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{toothpick}^{1} \int_{toothpick}^{orangutang} \int_{toothpick}^{snowflake} \frac{toothpick^{compassrose+1}}{(compassrose+1)!} riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d campfire \cdots d orangutang d toothpick \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(1-toothpick\right)^{pinecone-(compassrose+1)}}{(pinecone-(compassrose+1))!} \frac{toothpick^{compassrose+1}}{(compassrose+1)!} riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d toothpick \\
& =\frac{1}{pinecone!} \int_{0}^{1}\\binom{pinecone}{compassrose+1}\left(1-toothpick\right)^{pinecone-(compassrose+1)} toothpick^{compassrose+1} riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d toothpick .
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
whirlwind & =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{compassrose=0}^{pinecone-1}\\binom{pinecone}{compassrose+1}\left(1-toothpick\right)^{pinecone-(compassrose+1)} toothpick^{compassrose+1}\right) riverbank\left(toothpick\right) d toothpick \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{goldfinch=1}^{pinecone}\\binom{pinecone}{goldfinch}(1-peppermint)^{pinecone-goldfinch} peppermint^{goldfinch}\right) riverbank(peppermint) d peppermint \quad\left(\text { we set } goldfinch=compassrose+1 \text { and } peppermint=toothpick\right) \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(1-(1-peppermint)^{pinecone}\right) riverbank(peppermint) d peppermint
\end{aligned}
\]
since the sum is the binomial expansion of \( ((1-peppermint)+peppermint)^{pinecone} \) except that the \( goldfinch=0 \) term, which is \( (1-peppermint)^{pinecone} \), is missing. Hence \( whirlwind=\int_{0}^{1} riverbank(peppermint) blacksmith(peppermint) d peppermint \) where \( blacksmith(peppermint)=1-(1-peppermint)^{pinecone} \). Clearly \( 0 \leq blacksmith(peppermint) \leq 1 \) for \( 0 \leq peppermint \leq 1 \).

Solution 2. Fix \( pinecone \geq 0 \) and let \( \left(rainstorm, \ldots, tumbleweed\right) \) be chosen uniformly from \( [0,1]^{pinecone} \). Fix \( peppermint \in[0,1] \), and let \( seashell(peppermint) \) denote the expected value of
\[
arrowhead=peppermint-\max \left(\{0\} \cup\left(\left\{rainstorm, \ldots, tumbleweed\right\} \cap[0, peppermint]\right)\right) .
\]

Let \( \left(butterfly, \ldots, orangutang\right) \) be the permutation of \( \left(rainstorm, \ldots, tumbleweed\right) \) such that \( butterfly \leq \cdots \leq orangutang \). The distribution of \( \left(butterfly, \ldots, orangutang\right) \) equals the distribution in the problem. Define
\[
moonstone=\sum_{compassrose=0}^{pinecone-1}\left(nightingale-marshmallow\right) riverbank\left(nightingale\right)
\]
where \( marshmallow \) is the largest \( honeycomb \) less than \( nightingale \), or 0 if no such \( honeycomb \) exists. The terms in \( moonstone \) are a permutation of those in the original Riemann sum (ignoring the final term in the Riemann sum, which is zero), so the expected values of the Riemann sum and of \( moonstone \) are equal. Each term in \( moonstone \) has expected value \( \int_{0}^{1} sunflower(peppermint) riverbank(peppermint) d peppermint \), since the expected value of \( nightingale-marshmallow \) conditioned on the event \( nightingale=peppermint \) for some \( peppermint \in[0,1] \) is the definition of \( sunflower(peppermint) \) using \( \left(honeycomb\right)_{goldfinch \neq compassrose+1} \in[0,1]^{pinecone-1} \). Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is \( pinecone \int_{0}^{1} sunflower(peppermint) riverbank(peppermint) d peppermint \).

It remains to determine \( seashell(peppermint) \) for \( pinecone \geq 0 \). Let \( starflower \) be chosen uniformly in \( [0,1] \), independently of the \( rainstorm, \ldots, tumbleweed \) in the definition of \( seashell(peppermint) \). Then \( seashell(peppermint) \) equals the probability that \( starflower \) is in \( [0, peppermint] \) and is closer to \( peppermint \) than any other \( honeycomb \), since this probability conditioned on a choice of \( rainstorm, \ldots, tumbleweed \) equals \( arrowhead \). On the other hand, this probability equals \( \left(1-(1-peppermint)^{pinecone+1}\right) /(pinecone+1) \), since the probability that at least one of \( rainstorm, \ldots, starflower \) lies in \( [0, peppermint] \) equals \( 1-(1-peppermint)^{pinecone+1} \), and conditioned on this, the probability that the \( honeycomb \) in \( [0, peppermint] \) closest to \( peppermint \) is \( starflower \) is \( 1 /(pinecone+1) \), since all possible indices for this closest \( honeycomb \) are equally likely. Thus \( seashell(peppermint)=\left(1-(1-peppermint)^{pinecone+1}\right) /(pinecone+1) \), and we may take \( blacksmith(peppermint)=pinecone sunflower(peppermint)=1-(1-peppermint)^{pinecone} \).

Literature note. For more on Riemann sums, see [Spv, Ch. 13, Appendix 1].

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $(lastcoordinate,\,middlecoordinate,\,\ldots\,firstcoordinate)$ be a point chosen at random from the
singulardim-dimensional region defined by
$0<lastcoordinate<middlecoordinate<\cdots < firstcoordinate<1.$  Let $discretefunct$ be a continuous function on
$[0,1]$ with $discretefunct(1)=0$.
Set $finalpoint=0$ and $zerocoordinate=1$. Show that the expected value of the
Riemann sum
\[
\sum_{fixedindex=0}^{singulardim} (previouscoordinate-fixedcoordinate) \,discretefunct(previouscoordinate)
\]
is $\int_0^1 discretefunct(staticvalue)\,irrationalcurve(staticvalue)\, dstaticvalue$, where $irrationalcurve$ is a polynomial of degree $singulardim$,
independent of $discretefunct$, with $0\le irrationalcurve(staticvalue)\le 1$ for $0\le staticvalue \le 1$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution 1. We may drop the \( fixedindex=singulardim \) term in the Riemann sum, since \( discretefunct\!\left(zerocoordinate\right)=discretefunct(1)=0 \). The volume of the region \( 0<lastcoordinate<\cdots<firstcoordinate<1 \) in \( \mathbb{R}^{singulardim} \) is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{firstcoordinate} \cdots \int_{0}^{middlecoordinate} d lastcoordinate \, d middlecoordinate \cdots d firstcoordinate =\frac{1}{singulardim!}.
\]

Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is
\[
observedvalue=\left(\sum_{fixedindex=0}^{singulardim-1} \macrovalue_{fixedindex}\right)\Big/\left(\frac1{singulardim!}\right),
\]
where
\[
\begin{aligned}
\macrovalue_{fixedindex} & =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{firstcoordinate} \cdots \int_{0}^{middlecoordinate}\left(previouscoordinate-fixedcoordinate\right) \,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d lastcoordinate \cdots d firstcoordinate \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{firstcoordinate} \cdots \int_{0}^{previouscoordinate}\left(previouscoordinate-fixedcoordinate\right) \frac{fixedcoordinate^{fixedindex-1}}{(fixedindex-1)!}\,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d fixedcoordinate \,d previouscoordinate \cdots d firstcoordinate \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{firstcoordinate} \cdots \int_{0}^{earliercoordinate}\left(previouscoordinate \frac{previouscoordinate^{fixedindex}}{fixedindex!}-\frac{previouscoordinate^{fixedindex+1}}{(fixedindex+1)\,(fixedindex-1)!}\right) \,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d previouscoordinate \cdots d firstcoordinate \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{firstcoordinate} \cdots \int_{0}^{earliercoordinate} \frac{previouscoordinate^{fixedindex+1}}{(fixedindex+1)!}\,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d previouscoordinate \cdots d firstcoordinate \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{previouscoordinate}^{1} \int_{previouscoordinate}^{firstcoordinate} \int_{previouscoordinate}^{ancientcoordinate} \frac{previouscoordinate^{fixedindex+1}}{(fixedindex+1)!}\,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d earliercoordinate \cdots d firstcoordinate \,d previouscoordinate \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(1-previouscoordinate\right)^{singulardim-(fixedindex+1)}}{(singulardim-(fixedindex+1))!} \frac{previouscoordinate^{fixedindex+1}}{(fixedindex+1)!}\,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d previouscoordinate \\
& =\frac{1}{singulardim!} \int_{0}^{1}\binom{singulardim}{fixedindex+1}\left(1-previouscoordinate\right)^{singulardim-(fixedindex+1)} previouscoordinate^{fixedindex+1}\,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d previouscoordinate .
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
observedvalue & =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{fixedindex=0}^{singulardim-1}\binom{singulardim}{fixedindex+1}\left(1-previouscoordinate\right)^{singulardim-(fixedindex+1)} previouscoordinate^{fixedindex+1}\right)\,discretefunct\!\left(previouscoordinate\right) \,d previouscoordinate \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{stableindex=1}^{singulardim}\binom{singulardim}{stableindex}(1-staticvalue)^{singulardim-stableindex} staticvalue^{stableindex}\right)\,discretefunct(staticvalue) \,d staticvalue \quad\left(\text { we set } stableindex=fixedindex+1 \text { and } staticvalue=previouscoordinate\right) \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(1-(1-staticvalue)^{singulardim}\right)\,discretefunct(staticvalue) \,d staticvalue ,
\end{aligned}
\]
since the sum is the binomial expansion of \( ((1-staticvalue)+staticvalue)^{singulardim} \) except that the \( stableindex=0 \) term, which is \( (1-staticvalue)^{singulardim} \), is missing. Hence \( observedvalue=\int_{0}^{1} \discretefunct(staticvalue)\, \irrationalcurve(staticvalue)\, d staticvalue \) where \( \irrationalcurve(staticvalue)=1-(1-staticvalue)^{singulardim} \). Clearly \( 0 \leq \irrationalcurve(staticvalue) \leq 1 \) for \( 0 \leq staticvalue \leq 1 \).

Solution 2. Fix \( singulardim \geq 0 \) and let \( \left(outsidepoint, \ldots, boundarypoint\right) \) be chosen uniformly from \( [0,1]^{singulardim} \). Fix \( staticvalue \in[0,1] \), and let \( randomnessvalue_{singulardim}(staticvalue) \) denote the expected value of
\[
steadysegment_{singulardim}=staticvalue-\max \left(\{0\} \cup\left(\left\{outsidepoint, \ldots, boundarypoint\right\} \cap[0, staticvalue]\right)\right) .
\]

Let \( \left(lastcoordinate, \ldots, firstcoordinate\right) \) be the permutation of \( \left(outsidepoint, \ldots, boundarypoint\right) \) such that \( lastcoordinate \leq \cdots \leq firstcoordinate \). The distribution of \( \left(lastcoordinate, \ldots, firstcoordinate\right) \) equals the distribution in the problem. Define
\[
voidness=\sum_{fixedindex=0}^{singulardim-1}\left(precedingpoint-summitpoint\right)\, \discretefunct\!\left(precedingpoint\right)
\]
where \( summitpoint \) is the largest \( genericpoint \) less than \( precedingpoint \), or 0 if no such \( genericpoint \) exists. The terms in \( voidness \) are a permutation of those in the original Riemann sum (ignoring the final term in the Riemann sum, which is zero), so the expected values of the Riemann sum and of \( voidness \) are equal. Each term in \( voidness \) has expected value \( \int_{0}^{1} \unpredictvalue(staticvalue) \,\discretefunct(staticvalue) \,d staticvalue \), since the expected value of \( precedingpoint-summitpoint \) conditioned on the event \( precedingpoint=staticvalue \) for some \( staticvalue \in[0,1] \) is the definition of \( \unpredictvalue(staticvalue) \) using \( \left(genericpoint\right)_{genericpoint \neq precedingpoint} \in[0,1]^{singulardim-1} \). Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is \( singulardim \int_{0}^{1} \unpredictvalue(staticvalue)\, \discretefunct(staticvalue) \,d staticvalue \).

It remains to determine \( randomnessvalue_{singulardim}(staticvalue) \) for \( singulardim \geq 0 \). Let \( exteriorpoint \) be chosen uniformly in \( [0,1] \), independently of the \( outsidepoint, \ldots, boundarypoint \) in the definition of \( randomnessvalue_{singulardim}(staticvalue) \). Then \( randomnessvalue_{singulardim}(staticvalue) \) equals the probability that \( exteriorpoint \) is in \( [0, staticvalue] \) and is closer to \( staticvalue \) than any other \( genericpoint \), since this probability conditioned on a choice of \( outsidepoint, \ldots, boundarypoint \) equals \( steadysegment_{singulardim} \). On the other hand, this probability equals \( \left(1-(1-staticvalue)^{singulardim+1}\right)/(singulardim+1) \), since the probability that at least one of \( outsidepoint, \ldots, exteriorpoint \) lies in \( [0, staticvalue] \) equals \( 1-(1-staticvalue)^{singulardim+1} \), and conditioned on this, the probability that the \( genericpoint \) in \( [0, staticvalue] \) closest to \( staticvalue \) is \( exteriorpoint \) is \( 1 /(singulardim+1) \), since all possible indices for this closest \( genericpoint \) are equally likely. Thus
\[
randomnessvalue_{singulardim}(staticvalue)=\frac{1-(1-staticvalue)^{singulardim+1}}{singulardim+1},
\]
and we may take \( \irrationalcurve(staticvalue)=singulardim \,\unpredictvalue(staticvalue)=1-(1-staticvalue)^{singulardim}. \)

Literature note. For more on Riemann sums, see [Spv, Ch. 13, Appendix 1].

garbled_string.question:
Let $(qvnsdryk,\,mzkplfau,\,\ldots\,wjdqopcz)$ be a point chosen at random from the
$vbanmcot$-dimensional region defined by
$0<qvnsdryk<mzkplfau<\cdots < wjdqopcz<1.$  Let $sirdmafe$ be a continuous function on
$[0,1]$ with $sirdmafe(1)=0$.
Set $hjgrksla=0$ and $pfjadkmu=1$. Show that the expected value of the
Riemann sum
\[
\sum_{dlmqyrnt=0}^{vbanmcot} (syctamuv-brghtnke) sirdmafe(syctamuv)
\]
is $\int_0^1 sirdmafe(gloxrime)ltqirhvm(gloxrime)\, dgloxrime$, where $ltqirhvm$ is a polynomial of degree $vbanmcot$,
independent of $sirdmafe$, with $0\le ltqirhvm(gloxrime)\le 1$ for $0\le gloxrime \le 1$.

\end{itemize}
\end{document}
garbled_string.solution:
Solution 1. We may drop the $(dlmqyrnt=vbanmcot)$ term in the Riemann sum, since $sirdmafe\left(pfjadkmu\right)=sirdmafe(1)=0$. The volume of the region $0<qvnsdryk<\cdots<wjdqopcz<1$ in $\mathbb{R}^{vbanmcot}$ is
\[
\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{wjdqopcz} \cdots \int_{0}^{mzkplfau} d qvnsdryk d mzkplfau \cdots d wjdqopcz=1 / vbanmcot!
\]

Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is $kxpvozlh=\left(\sum_{dlmqyrnt=0}^{vbanmcot-1} otpwlzfk\right) /(1 / vbanmcot!)$, where
\[
\begin{aligned}
otpwlzfk & =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{wjdqopcz} \cdots \int_{0}^{mzkplfau}\left(syctamuv-brghtnke\right) sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d qvnsdryk \cdots d wjdqopcz \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{wjdqopcz} \cdots \int_{0}^{syctamuv}\left(syctamuv-brghtnke\right) \frac{brghtnke^{dlmqyrnt-1}}{(dlmqyrnt-1)!} sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d brghtnke d syctamuv \cdots d wjdqopcz \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{wjdqopcz} \cdots \int_{0}^{lbhqveop}\left(syctamuv \frac{syctamuv^{dlmqyrnt}}{dlmqyrnt!}-\frac{syctamuv^{dlmqyrnt+1}}{(dlmqyrnt+1) \cdot(dlmqyrnt-1)!}\right) sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d syctamuv \cdots d wjdqopcz \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{0}^{wjdqopcz} \cdots \int_{0}^{lbhqveop} \frac{syctamuv^{dlmqyrnt+1}}{(dlmqyrnt+1)!} sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d syctamuv \cdots d wjdqopcz \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \int_{syctamuv}^{1} \int_{syctamuv}^{wjdqopcz} \int_{syctamuv}^{zrxwcldi} \frac{syctamuv^{dlmqyrnt+1}}{(dlmqyrnt+1)!} sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d lbhqveop \cdots d wjdqopcz d syctamuv \\
& =\int_{0}^{1} \frac{\left(1-syctamuv\right)^{vbanmcot-(dlmqyrnt+1)}}{(vbanmcot-(dlmqyrnt+1))!} \frac{syctamuv^{dlmqyrnt+1}}{(dlmqyrnt+1)!} sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d syctamuv \\
& =\frac{1}{vbanmcot!} \int_{0}^{1}\binom{vbanmcot}{dlmqyrnt+1}\left(1-syctamuv\right)^{vbanmcot-(dlmqyrnt+1)} syctamuv^{dlmqyrnt+1} sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d syctamuv .
\end{aligned}
\]

Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
kxpvozlh & =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{dlmqyrnt=0}^{vbanmcot-1}\binom{vbanmcot}{dlmqyrnt+1}\left(1-syctamuv\right)^{vbanmcot-(dlmqyrnt+1)} syctamuv^{dlmqyrnt+1}\right) sirdmafe\left(syctamuv\right) d syctamuv \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(\sum_{skvdohuf=1}^{vbanmcot}\binom{vbanmcot}{skvdohuf}(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot-skvdohuf} gloxrime^{skvdohuf}\right) sirdmafe(gloxrime) d gloxrime \quad\left(\text { we set } skvdohuf=dlmqyrnt+1 \text { and } gloxrime=syctamuv\right) \\
& =\int_{0}^{1}\left(1-(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot}\right) sirdmafe(gloxrime) d gloxrime
\end{aligned}
\]
since the sum is the binomial expansion of $((1-gloxrime)+gloxrime)^{vbanmcot}$ except that the $skvdohuf=0$ term, which is $(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot}$, is missing. Hence $kxpvozlh=\int_{0}^{1} sirdmafe(gloxrime) ltqirhvm(gloxrime) d gloxrime$ where $ltqirhvm(gloxrime)=1-(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot}$. Clearly $0 \leq ltqirhvm(gloxrime) \leq 1$ for $0 \leq gloxrime \leq 1$.

Solution 2. Fix $vbanmcot \geq 0$ and let $\left(aclwznot, \ldots, qerspfhc\right)$ be chosen uniformly from $[0,1]^{vbanmcot}$. Fix $gloxrime \in[0,1]$, and let $wvthuibc(gloxrime)$ denote the expected value of
\[
kejmpvla=gloxrime-\max \left(\{0\} \cup\left(\left\{aclwznot, \ldots, qerspfhc\right\} \cap[0, gloxrime]\right)\right) .
\]

Let $\left(qvnsdryk, \ldots, wjdqopcz\right)$ be the permutation of $\left(aclwznot, \ldots, qerspfhc\right)$ such that $qvnsdryk \leq \cdots \leq wjdqopcz$. The distribution of $\left(qvnsdryk, \ldots, wjdqopcz\right)$ equals the distribution in the problem. Define
\[
pshargwe=\sum_{dlmqyrnt=0}^{vbanmcot-1}\left(udnhqlej-nwsagxre\right) sirdmafe\left(udnhqlej\right)
\]
where $nwsagxre$ is the largest $hybkzuma$ less than $udnhqlej$, or 0 if no such $hybkzuma$ exists. The terms in $pshargwe$ are a permutation of those in the original Riemann sum (ignoring the final term in the Riemann sum, which is zero), so the expected values of the Riemann sum and of $pshargwe$ are equal. Each term in $pshargwe$ has expected value $\int_{0}^{1} rgkamcye(gloxrime) sirdmafe(gloxrime) d gloxrime$, since the expected value of $udnhqlej-nwsagxre$ conditioned on the event $udnhqlej=gloxrime$ for some $gloxrime \in[0,1]$ is the definition of $rgkamcye(gloxrime)$ using $\left(hybkzuma\right)_{skvdohuf \neq dlmqyrnt+1} \in[0,1]^{vbanmcot-1}$. Therefore the expected value of the Riemann sum is $vbanmcot \int_{0}^{1} rgkamcye(gloxrime) sirdmafe(gloxrime) d gloxrime$.

It remains to determine $wvthuibc(gloxrime)$ for $vbanmcot \geq 0$. Let $xpjvoitr$ be chosen uniformly in $[0,1]$, independently of the $aclwznot, \ldots, qerspfhc$ in the definition of $wvthuibc(gloxrime)$. Then $wvthuibc(gloxrime)$ equals the probability that $xpjvoitr$ is in $[0, gloxrime]$ and is closer to $gloxrime$ than any other $hybkzuma$, since this probability conditioned on a choice of $aclwznot, \ldots, qerspfhc$ equals $kejmpvla$. On the other hand, this probability equals $\left(1-(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot+1}\right) /(vbanmcot+1)$, since the probability that at least one of $aclwznot, \ldots, xpjvoitr$ lies in $[0, gloxrime]$ equals $1-(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot+1}$, and conditioned on this, the probability that the $hybkzuma$ in $[0, gloxrime]$ closest to $gloxrime$ is $xpjvoitr$ is $1 /(vbanmcot+1)$, since all possible indices for this closest $hybkzuma$ are equally likely. Thus $wvthuibc(gloxrime)=\left(1-(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot+1}\right) /(vbanmcot+1)$, and we may take $ltqirhvm(gloxrime)=vbanmcot\, rgkamcye(gloxrime)=1-(1-gloxrime)^{vbanmcot}$.

Literature note. For more on Riemann sums, see [Spv, Ch. 13, Appendix 1].

kernel_variant.question:
Let d\in \mathbb{N}, d\geq 1, and let n_1,\ldots ,n_d be fixed positive integers.  
For every r\in {1,\ldots ,d} choose a random point  

   (x_{r,1},\ldots ,x_{r,n_r})        (*)

uniformly (with respect to Lebesgue volume) from the simplex  

   0 < x_{r,1} < \cdots  < x_{r,n_r} < 2 .

The d choices are made independently and we set  

   x_{r,0}=0 , x_{r,n_r+1}=2  (r=1,\ldots ,d).

Fix a binary vector \varepsilon =(\varepsilon _1,\ldots ,\varepsilon _d)\in {0,1}^d and define the two boundary values  

   a_r := 0 if \varepsilon _r=0 ,   a_r := 2 if \varepsilon _r=1 .   (1)

Boundary condition.  
Throughout we work with integrable functions  

   f:[0,2]^d\to \mathbb{R} such that f(t)=0
   whenever t_r = a_r for at least one r.   (2)

(Thus f vanishes on the union of the 2d coordinate hyper-planes on which the ``chosen'' evaluation component touches the corresponding end-point.  
This hypothesis will be indispensable for the one-dimensional computation below.)

Define the \varepsilon -``mixed end-point'' Riemann sum

   S_{\varepsilon } :=  \Sigma _{i_1=0}^{n_1} \cdots  \Sigma _{i_d=0}^{n_d}
        (x_{1,i_1+1}-x_{1,i_1}) \cdots  (x_{d,i_d+1}-x_{d,i_d})
        f( x_{1,i_1+\varepsilon _1}, \ldots , x_{d,i_d+\varepsilon _d} ).   (3)

(a)  Prove that there exists a polynomial P_{\varepsilon }:[0,2]^d\to [0,1],
     independent of f, such that  

     E[S_{\varepsilon }] = \int _{[0,2]^d} f(t) P_{\varepsilon }(t) dt.     (4)

(b)  Determine this polynomial explicitly, prove 0\leq P_{\varepsilon }\leq 1 on [0,2]^d,
     and show the global Lipschitz estimate  

     |P_{\varepsilon }(t)-P_{\varepsilon }(s)| \leq  \frac{1}{2}(n_1+\cdots +n_d) \|t-s\|_1  (t,s\in [0,2]^d). (5)

(c)  For p\in [1,\infty ] define the linear functional  

     T_{\varepsilon ,n_1,\ldots ,n_d}: L^{p}([0,2]^d) \to  \mathbb{R},
      T_{\varepsilon ,n_1,\ldots ,n_d}(f) := E[S_{\varepsilon }]  (f subject to (2)).    (6)

     Prove that T_{\varepsilon ,n_1,\ldots ,n_d} is bounded on every L^{p} space and that  

     \|T_{\varepsilon ,n_1,\ldots ,n_d}\| = \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{L^{p'}([0,2]^d)},  p' := p/(p-1)  
     (with the usual convention p'=\infty  when p=1 and p'=1 when p=\infty ). (7)
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we write  

  N:=(n_1,\ldots ,n_d),  \phi _{R,n}(t):=1-(1-t/2)^{n},  \phi _{L,n}(t):=(1-t/2)^{n}.  

(All polynomial weights are evaluated for t\in [0,2].)

Step 0.  A one-dimensional computation with the correct boundary hypothesis  

Fix n\geq 1 and draw (x_1,\ldots ,x_n) uniformly from 0<x_1<\cdots <x_n<2.  
Put x_0:=0, x_{n+1}:=2.  For an integrable function g:[0,2]\to \mathbb{R} define the two Riemann sums  

 R_L(g) := \Sigma _{i=0}^{n} (x_{i+1}-x_i) g(x_i),     (8a)  
 R_R(g) := \Sigma _{i=0}^{n} (x_{i+1}-x_i) g(x_{i+1}).  (8b)

Important convention.  
When the left-endpoint version is needed we assume g(0)=0;  
for the right-endpoint version we assume g(2)=0.  
That hypothesis will eliminate the last term in (8a)/(8b) and, as we shall see, justifies the forthcoming integral identities - they are false without it.

Derivation of the right-endpoint weight \phi _{R,n}.  
Assume g(2)=0.  Rescale  

  y_i:=x_i/2  and  h(t):=g(2t).  

Then (8b) becomes  

  R_R(g)=2 \Sigma _{i=0}^{n} (y_{i+1}-y_i) h(y_{i+1})  with 0<y_1<\cdots <y_n<1.

Let \Delta _n:= {0<y_1<\cdots <y_n<1}.  Its Lebesgue volume is 1/n!.  Therefore

 E[R_R(g)] = 2\cdot n! \int _{\Delta _n} \Sigma _{i=0}^{n-1} (y_{i+1}-y_i) h(y_{i+1}) dy. (9a')

(The summation stops at i=n-1 because for i=n, x_{n+1}=2 and the term
vanishes by g(2)=0.)  
Proceed exactly as in the original derivation: fix i \leq  n-1, apply
Fubini, obtain

 \int _{\Delta _n} (y_{i+1}-y_i) h(y_{i+1}) dy
  = 1/[n!(i+1)] \int _{0}^{1} h(t) t^{\,i+1}(1-t)^{\,n-(i+1)} dt. (9b')

Summation over i=0,\ldots ,n-1 gives

 \Sigma _{i=0}^{n-1} t^{\,i+1}(1-t)^{\,n-(i+1)} = 1-(1-t)^{n}. (9c')

Putting the pieces together and reverting to t=2y yields

 E[R_R(g)] = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) [1-(1-t/2)^{n}] dt
       = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) \phi _{R,n}(t) dt. (10)

Derivation of the left-endpoint weight \phi _{L,n}.  
Now assume g(0)=0 and apply the previous result to the reflected function g(t):=g(2-t).  After the change of variables u:=2-t one obtains  

 E[R_L(g)] = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) (1-t/2)^{n} dt
       = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) \phi _{L,n}(t) dt. (11)

Elementary calculus gives, for both symbols *=L,R,  

 0\leq \phi _{*,n}(t)\leq 1,   |\phi '_{*,n}(t)| = n/2\cdot |(1-t/2)^{\,n-1}| \leq  n/2 (t\in [0,2]). (12)

Step 1.  Iterated expectation - proof of part (a)  

Fix all random coordinates belonging to r=2,\ldots ,d and denote their realisation by x^{(2:d)}.  
Define the auxiliary function depending on t_1\in [0,2]  

 g_1(t_1) := \Sigma _{i_2=0}^{n_2}\cdots \Sigma _{i_d=0}^{n_d}
      (x_{2,i_2+1}-x_{2,i_2})\cdots (x_{d,i_d+1}-x_{d,i_d})
      f(t_1, x_{2,i_2+\varepsilon _2},\ldots ,x_{d,i_d+\varepsilon _d}). (13)

Because of the boundary condition (2) we have g_1(a_1)=0, where a_1 is the corresponding end-point (0 or 2).  Hence the hypotheses of Step 0 are satisfied and we may use (10) if \varepsilon _1=1 and (11) if \varepsilon _1=0.  Conditioning on x^{(2:d)} gives  

 E[S_{\varepsilon } | x^{(2:d)} ] = \int _{0}^{2} g_1(t_1) \phi _{\varepsilon _1,n_1}(t_1) dt_1. (14)

Repeat the argument successively for r=2,3,\ldots ,d; each time Fubini and (10)/(11) insert the factor \phi _{\varepsilon _r,n_r}.  After d steps we arrive at

 E[S_{\varepsilon }] = \int _{[0,2]^d} f(t)  \Pi _{r=1}^{d} \phi _{\varepsilon _r,n_r}(t_r) dt. (15)

Thus statement (4) holds with  

 P_{\varepsilon }(t) := \Pi _{r=1}^{d} \phi _{\varepsilon _r,n_r}(t_r). (16)

Step 2.  Properties of P_{\varepsilon } - proof of part (b)  

Since every factor of (16) lies in [0,1], we immediately have 0\leq P_{\varepsilon }\leq 1.  
For its Lipschitz constant compute

 \partial _{t_r}P_{\varepsilon }(u) = \phi '_{\varepsilon _r,n_r}(u_r)\cdot \Pi _{q\neq r}\phi _{\varepsilon _q,n_q}(u_q),

hence, by (12),

 |\partial _{t_r}P_{\varepsilon }(u)| \leq  n_r/2 (u\in [0,2]^d).  

The mean-value theorem yields

 |P_{\varepsilon }(t)-P_{\varepsilon }(s)| \leq  \Sigma _{r=1}^{d} (n_r/2)|t_r-s_r|
          = \frac{1}{2}(n_1+\cdots +n_d)\|t-s\|_1  (t,s\in [0,2]^d), which is (5).

Step 3.  The operator norm - proof of part (c)  

Upper bound.  
For every f\in L^{p} subject to (2), Holder's inequality gives

 |T_{\varepsilon ,N}(f)| = |\int  fP_{\varepsilon }| \leq  \|f\|_{p}\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}, (17)

so \|T_{\varepsilon ,N}\| \leq  \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}.  

Lower bound - three cases, taking care of the boundary hyper-planes.  
Whenever we construct a test function that does not vanish on the
boundary, we modify it on that measure-zero set so that (2) holds
without changing its L^p norm or the value of the integral.

(i) 1<p<\infty .  
Define f_0(t):=sign P_{\varepsilon }(t)\cdot |P_{\varepsilon }(t)|^{p'-1}.  
After the above modification f_0 satisfies (2) and \|f_0\|_{p}=\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}^{p'/p}=\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}^{p'-1}.  Equality holds in (17), hence \|T\|\geq \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}.

(ii) p=\infty  (so p'=1).  
Take f(t)=sign P_{\varepsilon }(t) modified on the boundary.  Then \|f\|_{\infty }=1 and  
T(f)=\int |P_{\varepsilon }| = \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{1}, establishing equality.

(iii) p=1 (so p'=\infty ).  
Put M:=\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{\infty } and, for k\in \mathbb{N}, let  

 A_k := {t\in [0,2]^d : P_{\varepsilon }(t) \geq  M-1/k}.

Each A_k has positive measure, and \chi _{A_k} can be altered on the boundary so that (2) holds.  Set  

 f_k(t):=sign P_{\varepsilon }(t)\cdot \chi _{A_k}(t).

Then \|f_k\|_{1}=|A_k| and

 T(f_k)=\int _{A_k} |P_{\varepsilon }| \geq  (M-1/k)|A_k| = (M-1/k)\|f_k\|_{1}.

Hence \|T\| \geq  M-1/k for every k, so \|T\| \geq  M = \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{\infty }.  

Combining with the upper bound we have equality in all cases p\in [1,\infty ],
establishing (7).

The solution is now fully rigorous.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimensional interaction:  The problem replaces a single random
    partition with d independent partitions, forcing the solver to pass
    from one–dimensional Dirichlet/Beta reasoning to genuine
    multi-variable considerations and to keep careful track of indices.

2.  Mixed endpoints:  Allowing an arbitrary ε∈{0,1}^d means that both left-
    and right–endpoint Riemann sums occur simultaneously, so two distinct
    polynomial kernels have to be derived and then combined.

3.  Additional quantitative requirement:  Besides computing P_{ε},
    the solver must establish the global Lipschitz bound (3) and determine
    the exact operator norm (5).  These estimates are absent from both the
    original problem and the existing kernel variant and require a
    delicate derivative analysis and a functional-analytic argument.

4.  Depth of techniques:  The solution demands
       •  repeated use of the Beta–function computation from the classical
          one-dimensional case;
       •  factorisation of expectations over independent random partitions;
       •  multivariate calculus for the Lipschitz constant;
       •  Hölder duality to compute the operator norm.
    No step can be disposed of by simple pattern matching; each needs
    explicit, coordinated reasoning about several interacting concepts.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially more intricate, both
combinatorially and analytically, than either the original problem or the
current kernel variant.

Step 0.  A one-dimensional computation with the correct boundary hypothesis  

Fix n\geq 1 and draw (x_1,\ldots ,x_n) uniformly from 0<x_1<\cdots <x_n<2.  
Put x_0:=0, x_{n+1}:=2.  For an integrable function g:[0,2]\to \mathbb{R} define the two Riemann sums  

 R_L(g) := \Sigma _{i=0}^{n} (x_{i+1}-x_i) g(x_i),     (8a)  
 R_R(g) := \Sigma _{i=0}^{n} (x_{i+1}-x_i) g(x_{i+1}).  (8b)

Important convention.  
When the left-endpoint version is needed we assume g(0)=0;  
for the right-endpoint version we assume g(2)=0.  
That hypothesis will eliminate the last term in (8a)/(8b) and, as we shall see, justifies the forthcoming integral identities - they are false without it.

Derivation of the right-endpoint weight \phi _{R,n}.  
Assume g(2)=0.  Rescale  

  y_i:=x_i/2  and  h(t):=g(2t).  

Then (8b) becomes  

  R_R(g)=2 \Sigma _{i=0}^{n} (y_{i+1}-y_i) h(y_{i+1})  with 0<y_1<\cdots <y_n<1.

Let \Delta _n:= {0<y_1<\cdots <y_n<1}.  Its Lebesgue volume is 1/n!.  Therefore

 E[R_R(g)] = 2\cdot n! \int _{\Delta _n} \Sigma _{i=0}^{n-1} (y_{i+1}-y_i) h(y_{i+1}) dy. (9a')

(The summation stops at i=n-1 because for i=n, x_{n+1}=2 and the term
vanishes by g(2)=0.)  
Proceed exactly as in the original derivation: fix i \leq  n-1, apply
Fubini, obtain

 \int _{\Delta _n} (y_{i+1}-y_i) h(y_{i+1}) dy
  = 1/[n!(i+1)] \int _{0}^{1} h(t) t^{\,i+1}(1-t)^{\,n-(i+1)} dt. (9b')

Summation over i=0,\ldots ,n-1 gives

 \Sigma _{i=0}^{n-1} t^{\,i+1}(1-t)^{\,n-(i+1)} = 1-(1-t)^{n}. (9c')

Putting the pieces together and reverting to t=2y yields

 E[R_R(g)] = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) [1-(1-t/2)^{n}] dt
       = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) \phi _{R,n}(t) dt. (10)

Derivation of the left-endpoint weight \phi _{L,n}.  
Now assume g(0)=0 and apply the previous result to the reflected function g(t):=g(2-t).  After the change of variables u:=2-t one obtains  

 E[R_L(g)] = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) (1-t/2)^{n} dt
       = \int _{0}^{2} g(t) \phi _{L,n}(t) dt. (11)

Elementary calculus gives, for both symbols *=L,R,  

 0\leq \phi _{*,n}(t)\leq 1,   |\phi '_{*,n}(t)| = n/2\cdot |(1-t/2)^{\,n-1}| \leq  n/2 (t\in [0,2]). (12)

Step 1.  Iterated expectation - proof of part (a)  

Fix all random coordinates belonging to r=2,\ldots ,d and denote their realisation by x^{(2:d)}.  
Define the auxiliary function depending on t_1\in [0,2]  

 g_1(t_1) := \Sigma _{i_2=0}^{n_2}\cdots \Sigma _{i_d=0}^{n_d}
      (x_{2,i_2+1}-x_{2,i_2})\cdots (x_{d,i_d+1}-x_{d,i_d})
      f(t_1, x_{2,i_2+\varepsilon _2},\ldots ,x_{d,i_d+\varepsilon _d}). (13)

Because of the boundary condition (2) we have g_1(a_1)=0, where a_1 is the corresponding end-point (0 or 2).  Hence the hypotheses of Step 0 are satisfied and we may use (10) if \varepsilon _1=1 and (11) if \varepsilon _1=0.  Conditioning on x^{(2:d)} gives  

 E[S_{\varepsilon } | x^{(2:d)} ] = \int _{0}^{2} g_1(t_1) \phi _{\varepsilon _1,n_1}(t_1) dt_1. (14)

Repeat the argument successively for r=2,3,\ldots ,d; each time Fubini and (10)/(11) insert the factor \phi _{\varepsilon _r,n_r}.  After d steps we arrive at

 E[S_{\varepsilon }] = \int _{[0,2]^d} f(t)  \Pi _{r=1}^{d} \phi _{\varepsilon _r,n_r}(t_r) dt. (15)

Thus statement (4) holds with  

 P_{\varepsilon }(t) := \Pi _{r=1}^{d} \phi _{\varepsilon _r,n_r}(t_r). (16)

Step 2.  Properties of P_{\varepsilon } - proof of part (b)  

Since every factor of (16) lies in [0,1], we immediately have 0\leq P_{\varepsilon }\leq 1.  
For its Lipschitz constant compute

 \partial _{t_r}P_{\varepsilon }(u) = \phi '_{\varepsilon _r,n_r}(u_r)\cdot \Pi _{q\neq r}\phi _{\varepsilon _q,n_q}(u_q),

hence, by (12),

 |\partial _{t_r}P_{\varepsilon }(u)| \leq  n_r/2 (u\in [0,2]^d).  

The mean-value theorem yields

 |P_{\varepsilon }(t)-P_{\varepsilon }(s)| \leq  \Sigma _{r=1}^{d} (n_r/2)|t_r-s_r|
          = \frac{1}{2}(n_1+\cdots +n_d)\|t-s\|_1  (t,s\in [0,2]^d), which is (5).

Step 3.  The operator norm - proof of part (c)  

Upper bound.  
For every f\in L^{p} subject to (2), Holder's inequality gives

 |T_{\varepsilon ,N}(f)| = |\int  fP_{\varepsilon }| \leq  \|f\|_{p}\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}, (17)

so \|T_{\varepsilon ,N}\| \leq  \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}.  

Lower bound - three cases, taking care of the boundary hyper-planes.  
Whenever we construct a test function that does not vanish on the
boundary, we modify it on that measure-zero set so that (2) holds
without changing its L^p norm or the value of the integral.

(i) 1<p<\infty .  
Define f_0(t):=sign P_{\varepsilon }(t)\cdot |P_{\varepsilon }(t)|^{p'-1}.  
After the above modification f_0 satisfies (2) and \|f_0\|_{p}=\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}^{p'/p}=\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}^{p'-1}.  Equality holds in (17), hence \|T\|\geq \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{p'}.

(ii) p=\infty  (so p'=1).  
Take f(t)=sign P_{\varepsilon }(t) modified on the boundary.  Then \|f\|_{\infty }=1 and  
T(f)=\int |P_{\varepsilon }| = \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{1}, establishing equality.

(iii) p=1 (so p'=\infty ).  
Put M:=\|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{\infty } and, for k\in \mathbb{N}, let  

 A_k := {t\in [0,2]^d : P_{\varepsilon }(t) \geq  M-1/k}.

Each A_k has positive measure, and \chi _{A_k} can be altered on the boundary so that (2) holds.  Set  

 f_k(t):=sign P_{\varepsilon }(t)\cdot \chi _{A_k}(t).

Then \|f_k\|_{1}=|A_k| and

 T(f_k)=\int _{A_k} |P_{\varepsilon }| \geq  (M-1/k)|A_k| = (M-1/k)\|f_k\|_{1}.

Hence \|T\| \geq  M-1/k for every k, so \|T\| \geq  M = \|P_{\varepsilon }\|_{\infty }.  

Combining with the upper bound we have equality in all cases p\in [1,\infty ],
establishing (7).

The solution is now fully rigorous.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1.  Higher dimensional interaction:  The problem replaces a single random
    partition with d independent partitions, forcing the solver to pass
    from one–dimensional Dirichlet/Beta reasoning to genuine
    multi-variable considerations and to keep careful track of indices.

2.  Mixed endpoints:  Allowing an arbitrary ε∈{0,1}^d means that both left-
    and right–endpoint Riemann sums occur simultaneously, so two distinct
    polynomial kernels have to be derived and then combined.

3.  Additional quantitative requirement:  Besides computing P_{ε},
    the solver must establish the global Lipschitz bound (3) and determine
    the exact operator norm (5).  These estimates are absent from both the
    original problem and the existing kernel variant and require a
    delicate derivative analysis and a functional-analytic argument.

4.  Depth of techniques:  The solution demands
       •  repeated use of the Beta–function computation from the classical
          one-dimensional case;
       •  factorisation of expectations over independent random partitions;
       •  multivariate calculus for the Lipschitz constant;
       •  Hölder duality to compute the operator norm.
    No step can be disposed of by simple pattern matching; each needs
    explicit, coordinated reasoning about several interacting concepts.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially more intricate, both
combinatorially and analytically, than either the original problem or the
current kernel variant.
